did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses the YOLOv8 deep learning object detection model to detect and localize lesions of Atopic Dermatitis and Psoriasis in medical images. The study evaluates the model's performance using metrics such as Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and mAP@0.5 under varying lighting conditions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from a study conducted at the 2025 International Conference on Data Science and Its Applications (ICoDSA). It describes a specific methodology using the YOLOv8 model on two datasets (with and without data augmentation) to detect Atopic Dermatitis and Psoriasis. The paper reports new quantitative results (Precision, Recall, F1-Score, mAP@0.5) and discusses the model's performance, which characterizes it as a research paper rather than a review.

title: Enhancing YOLOv8 for Robust Detection of Atopic Dermatitis and Psoriasis Under Uncontrolled Lighting Conditions
journal: 2025 International Conference on Data Science and Its Applications (ICoDSA)
brief_summary: This study evaluates the YOLOv8 object detection model for simultaneously detecting Atopic Dermatitis and Psoriasis lesions under uncontrolled lighting conditions. The researchers utilized two datasets: a baseline of 1,990 images and an augmented dataset of 5,412 images created using flipping, brightness, and exposure adjustments to simulate real-world variability. Model performance was assessed using Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and mAP@0.5 metrics on a split of training, validation, and testing subsets. The non-augmented model achieved 89.9% Precision and 94.7% mAP@0.5, while the augmented model improved to 94.4% Precision, 92.3% Recall, and 96.6% mAP@0.5. The results demonstrate that data augmentation significantly enhances YOLOv8's robustness and generalization for dermatological detection in diverse lighting environments.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two primary classes detected by the YOLOv8 model. The dataset contained 990 images of atopic dermatitis in the non-augmented set and was part of the augmented set.
One of the two primary classes detected by the YOLOv8 model. The dataset contained 1,000 images of psoriasis in the non-augmented set and was part of the augmented set.

all_task_types.target: Bounding boxes and class labels for Atopic Dermatitis and Psoriasis lesions
all_task_types.details: The paper uses YOLOv8 for object detection to simultaneously detect and localize lesions of Atopic Dermatitis and Psoriasis. The model predicts bounding boxes around the lesions and classifies them into one of two discrete categories (Atopic Dermatitis or Psoriasis). Performance is evaluated using object detection metrics including Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and mean Average Precision at IoU threshold 0.5 (mAP@0.5).

datasets_used.name: Dataset Without Augmentation
datasets_used.description: A baseline dataset of 1,990 medical images collected from various public repositories and medical image databases. Images were captured using smartphones and consumer-grade cameras to simulate real-world clinical scenarios with diverse image quality, resolution, and lighting conditions.

use.training: Used to train the YOLOv8-M model in the first scenario (baseline) without data augmentation.
use.validation: Used for validation during the first scenario training.
use.testing: Used for testing the baseline model performance.
use.other: Served as the source for the augmented dataset.

datasets_used.name: Dataset With Augmentation
datasets_used.description: An augmented dataset consisting of 5,412 images. It includes the original 1,990 images from the baseline dataset plus additional images generated via data augmentation techniques (flipping, brightness adjustments between -25% and +25%, exposure modifications between -10% and +10%, and saturation changes) to simulate diverse lighting conditions and object positioning.

use.training: Used to train the YOLOv8-M model in the second scenario to enhance robustness and generalization.
use.validation: Used for validation during the second scenario training.
use.testing: Used for testing the augmented model performance.
use.other: Used to evaluate the effectiveness of data augmentation in improving model robustness under varying lighting conditions.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: mean Average Precision at Intersection over Union threshold 0.5

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between the non-augmented and augmented datasets were made using descriptive statistics (Precision, Recall, F1-Score, mAP@0.5) without statistical significance testing.

Dataset Without Augmentation (First Scen.description: Baseline dataset of 1,990 images (990 Atopic Dermatitis, 1,000 Psoriasis) split into 70% training, 20% validation, 10% testing. Model: YOLOv8-M trained with Adam optimizer, learning rate 0.001, batch size 16, 100 epochs.
Dataset Without Augmentation (First Scen.notes: The model performed effectively in detecting lesions with high precision and recall. The mAP@0.5 indicates strong localization ability. Results are presented in Table II of the paper.

Dataset With Augmentation (Second Scenar.description: Augmented dataset of 5,412 images (original 1,990 + augmented) split into 70% training, 20% validation, 10% testing. Augmentations included flipping, brightness adjustments (-25% to +25%), exposure modifications (-10% to +10%), and saturation changes. Model: YOLOv8-M with same hyperparameters.
Dataset With Augmentation (Second Scenar.notes: Data augmentation significantly improved performance. Precision increased from 89.9% to 94.4%, Recall from 90.5% to 92.3%, F1-Score from 90.4% to 93.4%, and mAP@0.5 from 94.7% to 96.6%. The model became more sensitive to subtle lesions and improved localization accuracy. Results are presented in Table III of the paper.

architecture_used.main_model: YOLOv8-M (You Only Look Once, version 8, Medium variant)
architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported, but described as a state-of-the-art real-time object detection model with high efficiency and accuracy.

architecture_details.task_type: Object Detection (simultaneous detection and classification of Atopic Dermatitis and Psoriasis lesions)
architecture_details.preprocessing: Images resized to 640x640 pixels; auto-orient technique used to correct upside-down or tilted images.

training_details.hardware: Google Colab with NVIDIA Tesla T4 GPU

evaluation_metrics: mAP@0.5 (mean Average Precision at IoU threshold 0.5)

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to 640x640 pixels to meet YOLOv8 input requirements
Auto-orient technique to correct upside-down or tilted images
Data augmentation including flipping (horizontal or vertical)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Horizontal or vertical flipping applied to simulate variations in object positioning.
Adjustment of color intensity between -25% and +25%.
Adjustment of brightness between -25% and +25% to simulate brighter and darker lighting conditions.
Adjustment of exposure between -10% and +10% to simulate variations in lighting conditions.

conclusions: YOLOv8 is effective for detecting and localizing Atopic Dermatitis and Psoriasis lesions under uncontrolled lighting conditions.
Data augmentation significantly enhances the robustness and generalization of YOLOv8 for dermatological detection tasks.
Training on an augmented dataset (incorporating flipping, brightness, exposure, and saturation adjustments) improved model performance compared to a non-augmented baseline.
The augmented model achieved higher Precision (94.4% vs 89.9%), Recall (92.3% vs 90.5%), F1-Score (93.4% vs 90.4%), and mAP@0.5 (96.6% vs 94.7%) than the non-augmented model.

Data augmentation improved the model's ability to differentiate between lesions and non-lesion areas and reduced false negatives by exposing the model to a broader range of lighting conditions and object positions.
The improved localization accuracy (mAP@0.5) in the augmented model indicates better bounding box placement around lesions, which is critical for real-world clinical diagnosis and treatment.

The study addresses a gap in existing literature by demonstrating the combined detection of Atopic Dermatitis and Psoriasis using a single YOLOv8 framework under realistic, variable lighting conditions, rather than relying on controlled, high-quality datasets.
important_remarks: The study lacks external validation; the dataset was aggregated from various public repositories and medical image databases rather than a single controlled multi-center clinical trial, limiting generalizability.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the significance of performance improvements between the non-augmented and augmented models.
The dataset size is relatively small (1,990 baseline images), which may limit the robustness of the deep learning model compared to large-scale dermatological benchmarks.
The paper does not report the class-wise breakdown of performance metrics (Precision, Recall, F1, mAP) for Atopic Dermatitis versus Psoriasis separately, only providing aggregate results.

Data leakage risks are not explicitly addressed; while a 70/20/10 split is used, the source of images (public repositories) raises potential concerns about duplicate images across splits if not rigorously managed.

The study is limited to two specific skin conditions (Atopic Dermatitis and Psoriasis) and does not evaluate the model's ability to distinguish these from other similar dermatological conditions or healthy skin.

Deployment concerns are acknowledged but not empirically tested; the authors suggest future work on real-time diagnosis in low-resource settings, but no latency or inference speed metrics are reported in this study.

The reliance on simulated lighting variations via data augmentation (brightness, exposure, saturation) rather than capturing new real-world images with diverse lighting may not fully replicate the complexity of uncontrolled clinical environments.
